docs.title: 🌡️ Urban Heat Island Analysis in Spain
docs.text: This project analyses the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect in several Spanish cities using meteorological data and land use information.

docs.title: 📌 Objectives
docs.text:
• Compute UHI from urban and rural stations
  • Analyse differences between day and night
  • Study the influence of land use (CORINE)
  • Build regression models to explain UHI

docs.title: 📊 Results Overview
docs.text:
• UHI is generally higher at night
  • Urban areas show stronger heat retention
  • Vegetation reduces UHI

👉 See Analysis for more details.

docs.title: 🌡️ UHI Computation
docs.text:
The Urban Heat Island (UHI) is defined as:

UHI = T_urban - T_rural

The results show that:

  • UHI is consistently positive
  • Stronger during nighttime

docs.title: 🌙 Day vs Night
docs.text: Nighttime UHI is significantly higher due to: - Heat retention in urban materials - Reduced convection

docs.title: 🌍 Land Use Impact
docs.text:
Using CORINE Land Cover:

  • Higher % urban → higher UHI
  • Higher vegetation → lower UHI

docs.title: 📈 Regression Results
docs.text:
Linear models show:

  • Positive coefficient for urban areas
  • Negative coefficient for vegetation

docs.title: 🧠 Interpretation
docs.text: Urbanization increases heat storage, while vegetation mitigates temperature differences.

docs.title: Data Sources
docs.text:
• Meteostat: hourly temperature data
  • CORINE Land Cover: land use

docs.title: Preprocessing
docs.text:
• Missing values handled
  • Outliers removed
  • Data converted to Polars

docs.title: Land Use
docs.text:
• Buffers of 5 km around stations
  • Spatial intersection with CORINE
  • Percentage of land types computed

docs.title: Modeling
docs.text:
• Linear Regression
  • Ridge Regression

Variables used:

  • % urban
  • % vegetation
  • population
  • altitude difference

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• UHI is stronger at night
  • Urban areas intensify heat
  • Vegetation mitigates UHI

docs.title: Model Performance
docs.text:
• Linear model explains part of variance
  • Ridge improves stability
